title: Connection strings should not be vulnerable to injections attacks
description: Connection strings contain a series of parameters that are structured as key/value pairs, similar to key1=value1;key2=value2. If an attacker can control values that are inserted into the connection string, they may be able to insert additional parameters. These additional parameters can override values that were supplied earlier in the connection string.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

logic.detection_conditions: Detects string concatenation patterns that suggest building connection strings from user input
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when connection string keywords (Server, Database, User Id, etc.) are found in concatenated strings AND the concatenation involves variable input, excluding SqlConnectionStringBuilder usage and const/readonly values
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes SqlConnectionStringBuilder usage, const/readonly values, logging statements, non-connection-string concatenations, and configuration contexts
never_trigger_on: String concatenation not related to connection strings
logic.reasoning: Connection string injection occurs when user input is directly concatenated into connection strings, allowing attackers to inject additional parameters. The rule detects concatenation patterns with connection string keywords (Server, Database, User Id, Password, Data Source) combined with variables. SqlConnectionStringBuilder is excluded as it provides safe parameter handling. Const values are excluded as they cannot contain injected input. Logging and general string concatenation are excluded to avoid false positives.

should_not_trigger: var builder = new SqlConnectionStringBuilder { DataSource = "myServer", UserID = userId };
